summary: Jeff Atwood announces his decision to quit LinkedIn after two years of membership, arguing the service provides no tangible benefits to users. He criticizes the platform's difficult account deletion process and compares it to a chain letter or pyramid scheme. Despite maintaining his profile and selectively approving connection requests, he found zero value beyond unwanted headhunter calls. He concludes that LinkedIn primarily benefits LinkedIn itself, not its users, and urges others to close their accounts.
key_points: LinkedIn makes account deletion unnecessarily difficult, requiring a support ticket rather than offering a simple opt-out
Despite two years of active membership and selective networking, Atwood received no meaningful professional benefits
The service functions like a digital chain letter, building marketing value for LinkedIn while providing little to users
If you can only reach someone through LinkedIn, that itself signals the connection isn't worth pursuing
Users should critically evaluate who truly benefits from their participation in social networking services
money_quotes: Linked In is the digital equivalent of a chain letter.
If the only way you can reach someone is through this nutty online social pyramid scheme, you don't deserve to be taken seriously.
Consider carefully: who really benefits from your participation in Linked In? I'll tell you who benefits: Linked In.
Why build Linked In's marketing database with your valuable time and information?
I've found that there really is no there there.
It's high time we put an end to this glorified chain letter of a service.
key_insight: Social networking services that make it hard to leave while providing no clear user benefit are exploiting your data and time for their own gain.
